perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use good faith efforts (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In confidence and to take precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (b) not to divulge such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make use of such Proprietary Information except to consider internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy such Proprietary Information unless absolutely necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strict confidence' to 'confidence,' 'all reasonable precautions' to 'precautions,' 'any use whatsoever' to 'use of,' and 'not to copy or reverse engineer' to 'not to copy unless absolutely necessary' weakens the obligation. 'Good faith efforts' introduces an element of subjectivity, making it harder to enforce the confidentiality provisions stringently.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy to qualify for trade secret protection. Replacing 'strict confidence' and 'all reasonable precautions' with weaker language potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, endangering trade secret status. The 'unless absolutely necessary' clause introduces ambiguity. The California Trade secrets act has three requirements for misappropriation which include the information qualifies as a trade secret, the trade secret must be misappropriated by another, and the misappropriation must cause damages to the original trade secret holder.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken trade secret protection, potentially contradicting CUTSA's reasonable efforts requirement. Specifically, changing 'strict confidence' to 'confidence,' 'all reasonable precautions' to 'precautions,' and adding 'unless absolutely necessary' to copying creates uncertainty around the level of protection afforded to the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information. Snippet 1 states that 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy' constitutes 'improper means' of misappropriation under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) 3426.1. The changes weaken the obligation to maintain secrecy, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party at any time (which will be effective if actually received or three days after mailed first class postage prepaid to the Receiving Party’s address herein), the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party, or, at its sole discretion, destroy and certify to the Receiving party in writing the destruction of all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon a request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will endeavor to turn over to the Disclosing Party, or may choose to destroy, all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information if it is not feasible to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Promptly upon receipt of a written request' to 'Upon a request' removes the urgency. Changing 'will turn over' to 'will endeavor to turn over' makes the return of information less certain. Removing the part about certifying the destruction also is problematic. The phrase 'if it is not feasible to do so' is extremely vague and could be used as a loophole to avoid complying with the request altogether, regardless of state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: During discovery, failing to promptly and fully comply with a request for production of documents can be a violation of procedural rules. This section outlines requirements for responding to inspection demands, and the modified text introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as non-compliance, such as the duty to respond fully and not to withhold information that is requested.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2031.310 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.310. (a) On receipt of a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the demanding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) A statement of compliance with the demand is incomplete. (2) A representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. (3) An objection in the response is without merit or too general. (b) A motion under subdivision (a) shall comply with each of the following: (1) The motion shall set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the discovery sought by the demand. (2) The motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return proprietary information, creating a potential conflict with legal discovery requirements. The original text states the Receiving Party 'will turn over' information promptly, while the changed text uses 'will endeavor to turn over,' making the action less certain. Additionally, the original text requires certification of destruction, which is removed in the changed text. The phrase 'if it is not feasible to do so' is a vague loophole that could be used to avoid compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 2031.310(a), allows a party to move for an order compelling further response to a demand if a statement of compliance is incomplete or a representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. The changes introduce ambiguity and weaken the commitment to return information, potentially leading to disputes over compliance and requiring legal intervention to compel a complete response.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that a breach of this Agreement might cause harm, and therefore, the non-breaching party may consider seeking equitable relief in addition to remedies at law, if deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgement that there is no adequate remedy at law, the statement that a breach *may* lead to unfair competition, and stating that equitable relief can be considered 'if deemed necessary' weakens the enforceability of the agreement. It implies that equitable relief is not necessarily warranted, even for a material breach, making it more difficult to obtain an injunction or specific performance, contradicting established legal principles of remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 3422 outlines specific cases in which preventive relief (injunction) may be granted. By making equitable relief discretionary ('if deemed necessary'), the modified text undermines the grounds for seeking an injunction to prevent a breach of contract or further harm, potentially conflicting with the statutory basis for injunctive relief under California law, which may not require the additional hurdle introduced by the modified text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts CIV 3422. The original text ensures equitable relief for breaches, but the changed text makes it discretionary ('if deemed necessary'). CIV 3422 allows injunctions when 'pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief'. By making equitable relief optional, the changed text undermines the possibility of obtaining an injunction, directly conflicting with the circumstances outlined in CIV 3422 where such relief is typically available.

perturbation.original_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company should refrain from actively targeting key employees or executive officers of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had direct contact in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the consideration of applications received from such persons.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not solicit or seek to employ' to 'should refrain from actively targeting' significantly weakens the restriction on soliciting employees. Also, 'direct contact' is vague. The removal of clause (iii) regarding cessation of employment is important, because California law views restraints on employment after termination more negatively.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While the original text had exceptions, the modified text's vagueness (e.g., 'actively targeting,' 'direct contact') and weakened prohibition ('should refrain') creates uncertainty as to whether the no-hire clause is an unlawful restraint of trade. California courts narrowly construe exceptions to Section 16600 and any ambiguity may lead to unenforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original agreement regarding soliciting employees, creating ambiguity about whether it unlawfully restrains trade in violation of California Business and Professions Code § 16600. The original text stated that "the Company will not solicit or seek to employ," whereas the revised text states that the company "should refrain from actively targeting." This change weakens the obligation. Furthermore, the scraped snippet states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The altered language, 'should refrain from actively targeting' introduces ambiguity, and the snippet states this section "shall be read broadly...to void the application of any noncompete agreement...no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter."
